title: Homer The Vigilante

jokes.text: And our portable TV. - D'oh! / And my necklace. - Ah, that's no big loss.
jokes.setup: Family listing stolen items, expecting sympathy
jokes.punchline: Homer dismisses wife's priceless heirloom as 'no big loss'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - his casual dismissal of something precious reveals his obliviousness and insensitivity in a believable way

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom's priceless heirloom was stolen.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: It was passed down through four generations of my family.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No big loss.

jokes.text: You probably got a whole drawer full of'em. / Well, yes, I do, but they're all heirlooms too.
jokes.setup: Homer assumes Marge has many similar necklaces
jokes.punchline: Marge confirms she does but explains they're ALL heirlooms
jokes.explanation: Solid callback to Homer's dismissiveness, reveals the absurdity of having multiple 'priceless' heirlooms

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You have a lot of necklaces like that?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh yes, I have several just like this one.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: They're all heirlooms.

jokes.text: Homer: 'You had a stamp collection?'
jokes.setup: Bart mentions his stolen stamp collection
jokes.punchline: Homer is genuinely surprised his son has such a nerdy hobby
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective character beat - Homer's obliviousness to his own son's interests

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, someone stole my stamp collection.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Your stamp collection? Since when do you collect stamps?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Since forever, man. I've been working on it for years.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, I had no idea you were into that nerdy stuff. Good for you, boy.

jokes.text: Stamp collection? Ha-ha! / Ohh. Bart's pain is funny, but mine isn't.
jokes.setup: Nelson laughs at Bart's stamp collection loss
jokes.punchline: Bart realizes the cruel irony of being mocked by the show's resident bully
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-commentary on the show's own dynamics - Bart recognizing Nelson's role as the one who laughs at others' misfortune

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Ha-ha!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What's so funny?

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Your stamp collection is gone!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh man, I'm being mocked by the show's resident bully.

jokes.text: Can't talk. Robbed. Go hell.
jokes.setup: Ned tries to make friendly neighbor conversation
jokes.punchline: Homer's blunt, caveman-like dismissal
jokes.explanation: Homer's primitive communication style under stress - the economy is perfect, dropping articles like a caveman

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well howdy-doodly, Homer! Fine weather we're having!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Go away.

jokes.text: Flanders mentions stolen 'Shroud of Turin beach towels'
jokes.setup: Flanders listing his stolen items
jokes.punchline: Sacred religious relic turned into beach accessories
jokes.explanation: Absurd religious merchandise perfectly fits Flanders' character while being genuinely weird

dialogue.speaker: Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, they stole my weather vane, my sundial, my birdbath, and worst of all, they turned my sacred religious relic into a beach umbrella!

jokes.text: Curse you, magic beans! / Stop blaming the beans.
jokes.setup: Homer was supposed to buy insurance but clearly bought something else
jokes.punchline: Reveals he bought literal magic beans like Jack and the Beanstalk
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd Homer decision - instead of insurance, he bought fairy tale beans. Marge's matter-of-fact defense makes it funnier

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, did you buy the insurance like I asked?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Even better, Marge! I bought magic beans!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Magic beans? Homer, those aren't real!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Not real? Then how do you explain this?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The guy said if I plant these, a giant beanstalk will grow and I'll find treasure at the top!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you bought Jack and the Beanstalk beans instead of insurance!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, this is way better than insurance. Insurance doesn't have giants!

jokes.text: Well, there doesn't seem to be any pattern yet... but if I take this one and move it here... Hello! It almost looks like an arrow.
jokes.setup: Police chief analyzing crime scene locations on a map
jokes.punchline: His 'brilliant' detective work reveals the obvious pattern points to the police station
jokes.explanation: Classic Wiggum incompetence - takes him forever to see an obvious pattern, then immediately panics

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Now let me just connect these dots here... and here... and here...

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Well, I'll be! All the crimes point directly to the police station!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: We've got a real mystery on our hands.

jokes.text: Hey, look, Chief! It's pointing right at this police station. / Let's get out of here! [All Screaming]
jokes.setup: Lou points out the arrow points at their own station
jokes.punchline: Instead of investigating, the entire police force flees in terror
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation from Wiggum's detective work - the logical conclusion is complete cowardice

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: Hey, the arrow's pointing at the police station!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Oh my God! It's pointing at us!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: All units, retreat! Scatter!

jokes.text: This reporter isn't saying that the burglar is an inhuman monster like the Wolfman... but he very well could be.
jokes.setup: TV reporter disclaiming he's not making wild accusations
jokes.punchline: Immediately makes exactly that wild accusation
jokes.explanation: Good satire of irresponsible journalism - the classic 'I'm not saying X, but...' followed immediately by saying X

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: I'm not making wild accusations, but the Springfield Nuclear Power Plant may be leaking radioactive waste into our water supply.

jokes.text: So, Professor, would you say it's time for everyone to panic? / Yes, I would, Kent.
jokes.setup: Reporter asks expert if it's time to panic
jokes.punchline: Expert matter-of-factly confirms yes, panic is appropriate
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery of absurd advice - the Professor's casual endorsement of panic is beautifully understated

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Is it time to panic?

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Yes, panic is appropriate.

jokes.text: Gosh, look at me. I'm sweatin' like a pig here. Ah. Ah, man. That's better.
jokes.setup: Wiggum about to demonstrate police dog tracking
jokes.punchline: Uses the evidence handkerchief to wipe his own sweat
jokes.explanation: Classic Wiggum incompetence - contaminating the key evidence before using it, completely oblivious

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Now, I'll use this evidence handkerchief to show how our police dog tracks a scent.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Phew, it's hot out here.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Well, that'll do it.

jokes.text: [Nervous Chuckle] Well, the real humans won't, uh, burn quite so fast in there.
jokes.setup: Salesman demonstrating home security system that apparently incinerated test dummies
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions real humans will burn slower
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about a security system that's more dangerous than burglars - the nervous delivery sells it

dialogue.speaker: Security Salesman
dialogue.line: Don't worry, the real humans burn much slower.

jokes.text: All right! Free laserium. All the colors of the 'bow, man.
jokes.setup: Laser security system activates
jokes.punchline: Otto thinks it's an intentional light show, shortens 'rainbow' in his stoner way
jokes.explanation: Perfect Otto character moment - finding joy in what others see as danger, with his characteristic slang

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Whoa, man! A light show! Rainbow! That's so cool, dude!

jokes.text: Ow! Oh. My cataracts are gone. I can see again. All the beauty of na-- Oh! I'm blind. Oh, well. Easy come, easy go.
jokes.setup: Laser hits elderly man's eyes
jokes.punchline: Brief moment of restored sight followed immediately by blindness, shrugged off casually
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark escalation - the roller coaster from blindness to sight to blindness, accepted with elderly resignation

dialogue.speaker: Jasper
dialogue.line: I can see! I can see! Oh wait, no I can't.

jokes.text: [Screams] It's the cat burglar! Please don't kill me! / Abe, can I borrow your ointment? / Oh, it's you, Malloy.
jokes.setup: Jasper screams in terror about the cat burglar
jokes.punchline: It's just his retirement home neighbor borrowing ointment
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection setup - the terror followed by mundane elderly interaction. Sets up Malloy as a character.

dialogue.speaker: Jasper
dialogue.line: A cat burglar! A cat burglar!

dialogue.speaker: Abe
dialogue.line: Relax, Jasper. It's just me borrowing some ointment.

jokes.text: All right. But this time clean off the applicator!
jokes.setup: Abe agrees to lend ointment
jokes.punchline: Reveals Malloy has borrowed it before and been unsanitary about it
jokes.explanation: Realistic elderly interaction - the gross but believable detail about sharing medicinal ointment

dialogue.speaker: Abe
dialogue.line: Sure, I'll lend you some ointment.

dialogue.speaker: Abe
dialogue.line: But I gotta warn you - Malloy borrowed it last week and he didn't wash his hands first. Or after. Or during, come to think of it.

jokes.text: [Gunshot] Thank you for coming. I'll see you in hell.
jokes.setup: Sounds like Malloy shot someone
jokes.punchline: He's actually just watching TV and commenting on a show
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - the gunshot sound effect makes us think violence, but it's just aggressive TV commentary

dialogue.speaker: Malloy
dialogue.line: He's actually just watching TV and commenting on a show

jokes.text: What, this jug? / [Rhythmic tones] Hee-hee! [Laughing] Whee! Whoa-ho-ho!
jokes.setup: Homer gives Lisa a jug to replace her stolen saxophone
jokes.punchline: Homer gets carried away playing the jug enthusiastically
jokes.explanation: Homer's childlike enthusiasm for simple pleasures - he's more excited about the jug than Lisa

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, I got you a replacement for your saxophone!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that's just a jug.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A jug? LISA, this is a MUSICAL JUG! Watch this!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ooh-ooh-ooh-ooh! Ooh-ooh-ooh-ooh!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, stop—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'M ON A ROLL NOW, SWEETIE! Ooh-ooh-ooh-ooh! OOOOOOH!

jokes.text: Lisa, never, ever stop in the middle of a hoedown.
jokes.setup: Lisa stops playing the jug
jokes.punchline: Homer treats jug-playing as a serious hoedown with unbreakable rules
jokes.explanation: Homer's absurd conviction about hoedown etiquette - treating casual jug playing as having sacred rules

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, you can't just stop playing the jug! We're in the middle of a hoedown!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But Dad, I don't want to play anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't want to? RULES ARE RULES, Lisa! Once you start a hoedown, you see it through to the end. That's the code of the hoedown!

jokes.text: Since the police can't seem to get off their duffaroonies... to do something about this burglareeno... I think it's time we start our own neighborhood watch... aroonie!
jokes.setup: Ned calls a neighborhood meeting about crime
jokes.punchline: His characteristic Flanders-speak with made-up cutesy word endings
jokes.explanation: Perfect Flanders characterization - his inability to say anything without cutesy word modifications, even when discussing crime

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, howdy-diddly-do, neighbors! We need to talk about the crime-a-rooney in our neighborhood. I've got a real humdinger-diddly of a plan to stop these criminal-a-diddlies!

jokes.text: Someone else! [All] Yea! Someone else! Someone else! Someone else! / I'm someone else. / He's right!
jokes.setup: Crowd chants for 'someone else' to lead instead of Flanders
jokes.punchline: Homer qualifies himself by literally being 'someone else'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant literal interpretation - Homer's logic is technically correct, and the crowd's acceptance of this logic is perfectly absurd

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Someone else! Someone else!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm someone else.

jokes.text: We don't need a thinker. We need a doer, someone who'll act without considering the consequences.
jokes.setup: Moe explains why Homer should lead
jokes.punchline: Lists 'acting without considering consequences' as a positive leadership quality
jokes.explanation: Perfect satire of mob mentality - presenting recklessness as exactly what they need in a leader

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: You should be the leader, Homer. You got all the qualifications.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I do?

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Yeah. You're stupid, you're ignorant, and you act without considering the consequences.

jokes.text: I'm filled with piss and vinegar. At first I was just filled with vinegar.
jokes.setup: Barney uses the expression 'full of piss and vinegar'
jokes.punchline: Reveals he was literally just full of vinegar before adding piss
jokes.explanation: Clever literal interpretation of an idiom - Barney's alcoholic logic makes this believably absurd

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: I'm full of piss and vinegar!

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Well, I was full of vinegar before I added the piss.

jokes.text: Who chased the Irish out of Springfield Village in aught-four? Me! That's who. / And a fine job you did too.
jokes.setup: Abe brags about his historical accomplishments
jokes.punchline: Reveals he committed what sounds like ethnic cleansing, which Homer casually praises
jokes.explanation: Dark historical humor - Abe's casual racism from 'the old days' treated as normal accomplishment

dialogue.speaker: Abe
dialogue.line: I've done a lot of important things in my life, boy. Things that shaped history.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Really, Dad? Like what?

dialogue.speaker: Abe
dialogue.line: Well, back in '43, I helped clear out an entire village. Completely emptied it. Not a soul left.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, Dad! That's amazing! You're a real go-getter!

jokes.text: but you're a very old man now... and old people are useless. Aren't they? Aren't they? Huh? / [Giggling] Yes, they are! Yes, they are!
jokes.setup: Homer explains why Abe can't help
jokes.punchline: Uses baby talk to get his infant daughter to agree that old people are useless
jokes.explanation: Homer's casual cruelty to his father, made worse by involving his baby daughter in the mockery

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Your grandfather's too old to help us, Maggie.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Goo goo ga ga, aren't old people useless?

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: *sucks pacifier in agreement*

jokes.text: Marge! We're responsible adults. [Gunshot] Whoops. / If a group of responsible adults can't handle firearms in a responsible way-- [Gunshot] Sorry. [Multiple gunshots] Uh-oh. Me again. Sorry.
jokes.setup: Homer argues they can handle guns responsibly
jokes.punchline: Immediate series of accidental gunshots undermining his point
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation structure - each accidental gunshot further undermines Homer's argument about responsibility

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We can handle guns responsibly!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: See? Nothing bad is gonna—

dialogue.speaker: Various vigilantes
dialogue.line: *gunshots*

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: I'll be Cue Ball, Skinner can be Eight Ball... Barney will be Twelve Ball, and, Moe, you can be Cue Ball. / You're an idiot.
jokes.setup: Homer assigns pool ball code names
jokes.punchline: Assigns himself and Moe the same name, earning Moe's contempt
jokes.explanation: Homer's poor planning skills on display - can't even assign unique code names, earning Moe's characteristic blunt assessment

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay, from now on, we're using code names. I'll be... 8-Ball.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And you'll be... 8-Ball.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: We can't both be 8-Ball, you idiot!

jokes.text: See, it's a miniature version of the A-bomb. The government built it in the '50s to drop on beatniks.
jokes.setup: Herman shows off a weapon
jokes.punchline: Claims the government made mini A-bombs specifically to target beatnik counterculture
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd historical alternate reality - the idea of weaponizing nuclear technology against poets and artists

dialogue.speaker: Herman
dialogue.line: The government made mini A-bombs specifically to target beatnik counterculture.

jokes.text: Take that, Maynard G. Krebs!
jokes.setup: Homer fires the anti-beatnik weapon
jokes.punchline: References specific 1950s TV character who was a stereotypical beatnik
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the beatnik premise with specific cultural reference to Dobie Gillis character

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Take that, you beatnik!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And that's for Maynard G. Krebs!

jokes.text: It's not just 'a megaphone,' Dad. It's a Rapmaster 2000.
jokes.setup: Homer wants to borrow Bart's megaphone
jokes.punchline: Bart corrects him with the ridiculous brand name that makes it sound like a rap music device
jokes.explanation: Good product name parody - the absurd branding that makes a megaphone sound hip-hop related

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey Bart, can I borrow your megaphone?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It's not a megaphone, Dad. It's a MC Hammer Amplification System.

jokes.text: Move along there. [Gasps] It's Hammer! / Return to your homes and places of businesses. [Cheering]
jokes.setup: Homer using megaphone to direct people
jokes.punchline: Someone mistakes Homer for MC Hammer, crowd gets excited
jokes.explanation: Dated but effective celebrity mistaken identity gag - plays on MC Hammer's popularity and the Rapmaster 2000 reference

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Attention everyone! Over here!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd Member
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is that MC Hammer?!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: MC Hammer! MC Hammer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, no, I'm Homer Simpson—

dialogue.speaker: MC Hammer
dialogue.line: Yo, that ain't me, fool!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: STOP! Hammer time!

jokes.text: Hey, you! Where'd you get that saxophone? / Sears. / Get him!
jokes.setup: Homer spots someone with a saxophone
jokes.punchline: Person gives innocent answer but vigilantes attack anyway
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing vigilante overreach - perfectly reasonable answer ignored in favor of mob violence

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, what's with the saxophone?

dialogue.speaker: Person
dialogue.line: I'm just practicing for my school band recital.

dialogue.speaker: Vigilante 1
dialogue.line: A saxophone?! That's a weapon!

dialogue.speaker: Vigilante 2
dialogue.line: Get him!

dialogue.speaker: Innocent bystander
dialogue.line: No, wait—

jokes.text: No burning leaves without a permit! / I got one. / Too late!
jokes.setup: Homer confronts someone burning leaves
jokes.punchline: Despite having proper permit, Homer attacks anyway
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of vigilante power abuse - even legal compliance doesn't save you from Homer's 'justice'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey! You can't burn leaves here!

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: Actually, I have a permit.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A permit? That's never stopped me before!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: *attacks*

jokes.text: You'd better have a good reason for doing that, boy. / It makes me feel like a big man. / Let me check my reason list. Yep, it's on here.
jokes.setup: Wiggum questions Lou's actions
jokes.punchline: Lou's honest but terrible reason is apparently acceptable per Wiggum's official list
jokes.explanation: Brilliant bureaucratic absurdity - Lou's petty power trip is officially sanctioned, revealing systematic police corruption

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Lou, why did you do that?

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: Well Chief, I was just following the official police list of acceptable reasons.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: What reason?

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: I felt like it.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Oh, that's on the list. Carry on.

jokes.text: Hey, you're that drunken posse. Wow! Can I join ya? / I don't know. Can you swing a sack of doorknobs? / Can I! / You're in. Here's the sack. But you gotta supply your own knobs.
jokes.setup: Herman wants to join the vigilante group
jokes.punchline: Qualification is ability to swing doorknobs, which comes with bureaucratic requirements
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of vigilante violence with absurd BYOK (Bring Your Own Knobs) policy

dialogue.speaker: Herman
dialogue.line: I want to join your group.

dialogue.speaker: Vigilante Leader
dialogue.line: Well, the only qualification is the ability to swing a doorknob.

dialogue.speaker: Herman
dialogue.line: I can do that!

dialogue.speaker: Vigilante Leader
dialogue.line: Great, but you'll need to fill out these forms in triplicate, get three references, and pass a background check.

jokes.text: So I said to him, 'Look, buddy, your car was upside down when we got here. And as for your grandma, she shouldn't have mouthed off like that!'
jokes.setup: Homer telling story about their vigilante activities
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of violence against elderly woman for talking back
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation showing how far the vigilantes have gone beyond their original mission

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, we were like vigilantes. One time this old lady talked back to me, so I punched her in the gut.

jokes.text: I mean, if you're the police, who will police the police? / I dunno. Coast Guard?
jokes.setup: Lisa asks the classic question about who watches the watchers
jokes.punchline: Homer's completely random and nonsensical answer
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response to serious philosophical question - his random, confident answer reveals complete lack of understanding

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But Dad, who watches the Watchers?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh, Regis and Kathie Lee?

jokes.text: Literacy programs, preserving our beloved covered bridges... world domination. / World domination? / Oh, that might be a typo.
jokes.setup: Homer lists the vigilante group's expanded activities
jokes.punchline: Casually includes world domination, then dismisses it as possible typo
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from mundane civic activities to global conquest, with Homer's casual dismissal making it funnier

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We've expanded our activities. We're now doing neighborhood patrols, community outreach, world domination... oh wait, that's probably a typo.

jokes.text: [Thinking] Mental note: The girl knows too much.
jokes.setup: Lisa questions Homer about world domination
jokes.punchline: Homer considers his own daughter a security threat
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about Homer's power corruption - treating his daughter like a spy movie security risk

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, why would you want to take over the world?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, if I ruled the world, I could make donuts a food group and declare nap time a national holiday.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But Dad, wouldn't that be bad for everyone else?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's why you're a security threat, Lisa. You think too much.

jokes.text: Oh, people can come up with statistics to prove anything, Kent. Forty percent of all people know that.
jokes.setup: Homer responds to crime statistics
jokes.punchline: Dismisses statistics by citing a made-up statistic
jokes.explanation: Absolutely brilliant self-defeating logic - Homer disproves statistics by making up a statistic. This became a cultural touchstone quote.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Statistics? Pffft. Everyone knows that 4 out of 5 people make up 80% of the population.

jokes.text: Oh, Kent, I'd be lying if I said my men weren't committing crimes. / Hmm, touché.
jokes.setup: Kent asks about crimes being caused by the vigilantes
jokes.punchline: Homer admits it completely honestly, Kent accepts this as a reasonable response
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer honesty that accidentally disarms criticism - his frank admission somehow wins the exchange

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Are you saying the vigilantes are causing crimes?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, pretty much.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Well, that seems fair.

jokes.text: Hello, Homer, my arch nemesis. / Y'ello. / Uh...you do realize who this is. / Uh... Marge?
jokes.setup: Cat burglar calls Homer, announcing himself dramatically
jokes.punchline: Homer has no idea who it is, guesses it's his wife
jokes.explanation: Great deflation of villain dramatics - the cat burglar's theatrical self-presentation falls flat against Homer's obliviousness

dialogue.speaker: Cat Burglar
dialogue.line: Hello, Homer Simpson. This is the Cat Burglar.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The Cat Burglar? Who is this?

dialogue.speaker: Cat Burglar
dialogue.line: I just told you — the Cat Burglar!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look, pal, I don't know who you are, but if you're selling something, we're not interested.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait... is this Marge?

jokes.text: Homer! That's our stage manager! / Oh. Sorry. I'm a little nervous.
jokes.setup: Homer apparently attacks someone during the TV interview
jokes.punchline: It was just the stage manager, Homer casually apologizes
jokes.explanation: Homer's trigger-happy vigilante behavior extending to innocent bystanders, with his casual apology

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Homer, you just attacked someone on live television!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, that was just the stage manager. Sorry, buddy.

jokes.text: We'll be right back. / I get to say that!
jokes.setup: Cat burglar tries to end the TV segment
jokes.punchline: Kent Brockman asserts his TV host prerogatives
jokes.explanation: Good professional jealousy joke - even during a crisis, Kent protects his TV host catchphrases

dialogue.speaker: Cat Burglar
dialogue.line: That's all the time we have.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Not so fast! I'm the host of this show, and I decide when the segment ends!

jokes.text: Just stand around and don't steal anything.
jokes.setup: Abe offers to help catch the burglar
jokes.punchline: Homer's incredibly low expectations for his father's contribution
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer-Abe relationship humor - the bar for Abe's helpfulness is literally just 'don't commit crimes'

dialogue.speaker: Abe
dialogue.line: I'll help you catch that burglar, boy!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Thanks, Dad. Just try not to fall asleep or die.

jokes.text: Well, Mr. Cat Burglar. You'd like to get in here, wouldn't you? There's just one little problem-- thirty-six years ago, some lady gave birth to a man named HomerJ. Simp--
jokes.setup: Homer guards the museum, addressing the burglar
jokes.punchline: Gets distracted by telling his own life story in unnecessarily detailed way
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer ego - he can't just say 'I'm guarding this place' without making it an epic personal narrative

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Halt! You're under arrest for burglary! Now, it all started when I was born in Springfield General Hospital, where my father Abe was waiting in the maternity ward with my mother Marge—well, not Marge yet, she was Marge Bouvier then—anyway, my father was so excited he dropped a cigar on the floor, and the nurse, whose name was Edna, no wait, that was my teacher, anyway the nurse said...

dialogue.speaker: Burglar
dialogue.line: Thanks, pal.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, where are you going? I haven't even gotten to the part where I ate the poisoned blowfish!

jokes.text: Oh, my God! Underage kids drinking beer without a permit!
jokes.setup: Homer is supposed to be guarding the museum
jokes.punchline: Gets distracted by kids drinking, focuses on the permit violation rather than underage drinking
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer priorities - more concerned about bureaucratic permits than actual child welfare

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey! You kids can't drink in here!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Do you have a permit for that beverage?

jokes.text: 'Asleep at the switch'? I wasn't asleep. I was drunk!
jokes.setup: Homer reads newspaper criticism about falling asleep on duty
jokes.punchline: Corrects the record that he was drunk, not asleep, as if that's better
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - his indignation at being mischaracterized, preferring the 'truth' that he was drunk on duty

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I was not asleep! I was drunk!

jokes.text: You let me down, man! Now I don't believe in nothing no more. I'm going to law school. / No!
jokes.setup: Otto expresses disillusionment with Homer
jokes.punchline: His response to losing faith is to become a lawyer, which horrifies Homer
jokes.explanation: Perfect character reversal - the laid-back Otto becoming a lawyer is treated as the worst possible outcome

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Man, I've lost faith in everything. I'm gonna become a lawyer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A lawyer?!

jokes.text: Before, I was too old and no one wanted my help. Suddenly, look who comes to old Grampa with-- / Wait! Where are you going? Come back. I'll tell you.
jokes.setup: Abe starts a guilt trip about being ignored
jokes.punchline: Homer literally walks away mid-sentence, forcing Abe to abandon his speech
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer-Abe dynamic - Homer has no patience for Abe's self-pity, forcing him to get to the point

dialogue.speaker: Abe
dialogue.line: Nobody ever visits me anymore. You kids don't care about your old man. I sit in that home all day long, and does anyone—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh huh, yeah, that's great Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I gotta go.

dialogue.speaker: Abe
dialogue.line: Wait, I'm not finished! I was just getting to the part about my war stories and—

jokes.text: On one of my frequent trips to the ground... / I noticed Malloy wore sneakers... for sneaking!
jokes.setup: Abe explains his detective work
jokes.punchline: Euphemistically calls his falls 'trips to the ground' and makes obvious sneaker/sneaking connection
jokes.explanation: Good Abe characterization - his pride in making the most obvious possible connection between sneakers and sneaking

dialogue.speaker: Abe
dialogue.line: I've been doing some detective work. I call my falls 'trips to the ground.' It's a sneaker way of saying sneaking around.

jokes.text: There was something strange about the way he walked. Much more vertical than usual.
jokes.setup: Abe describes suspicious behavior he observed
jokes.punchline: The suspicious behavior is walking upright instead of hunched over
jokes.explanation: Brilliant elderly perspective - in a retirement home, good posture is suspicious behavior. Perfect Abe observation.

dialogue.speaker: Abe
dialogue.line: I saw something suspicious today. A man walking down the street completely upright, shoulders back, head held high.

dialogue.speaker: Abe
dialogue.line: I immediately called the police. That's not normal behavior.

jokes.text: And finally, Malloy, unlike most retired people... has the world's largest cubic zirconia on his coffee table.
jokes.setup: Abe lists his final piece of evidence
jokes.punchline: Treats having the stolen centerpiece exhibit as just unusual for retirees
jokes.explanation: Good understated delivery - the smoking gun evidence presented as merely 'unusual' retirement home decor

dialogue.speaker: Abe
dialogue.line: And finally, the stolen centerpiece exhibit... well, you know how us retirees are. We collect things.

jokes.text: Aw, Dad, I could kiss ya! / Kiss me right here. It's the only part that still has feeling.
jokes.setup: Homer expresses affection for his father
jokes.punchline: Abe directs him to kiss a specific spot due to his advanced age/health issues
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable but realistic aging humor - Abe's physical limitations turning affection awkward

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dad, I love you.

dialogue.speaker: Abe
dialogue.line: Oh, that's nice, boy. Now kiss me on the liver spot.

jokes.text: So, Mr. Malloy, it seems that the cat has been caught... by the very person that was trying to catch him. / How ironic.
jokes.setup: Kent points out the ironic situation
jokes.punchline: Malloy dryly agrees with the assessment
jokes.explanation: Weak exchange - stating the obvious irony without adding much comedic value

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Well, isn't this ironic? Here we are, reporting on a news story about the dangers of fake news.

dialogue.speaker: Malloy
dialogue.line: Yes, the irony is almost as thick as the makeup on your face.

jokes.text: So, you see, old people aren't so useless after all. / Malloy's old, and he outsmarted the lot of you. And I'm even older, and I outsmarted him!
jokes.setup: Abe makes a point about elderly usefulness
jokes.punchline: Uses the criminal as an example, then trumps it with his own superior age and success
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier ageism with logical escalation - using age as a competitive advantage rather than disability

dialogue.speaker: Abe
dialogue.line: We old folks are still useful! Why, just last week I caught a criminal.

dialogue.speaker: Abe
dialogue.line: But that's nothing compared to what I did in World War II — I shot down four enemy planes and seduced a general's daughter!

jokes.text: Selma, my dear, your lock of MacGyver's hair. / Ohh. Thank you. I'll add it to the ball.
jokes.setup: Malloy returns stolen personal items
jokes.punchline: Reveals Selma has a ball made of celebrity hair
jokes.explanation: Perfect Selma characterization - her creepy celebrity obsession revealed through casual mention of hair collection

dialogue.speaker: Malloy
dialogue.line: Here are your stolen personal items, Selma.

dialogue.speaker: Selma
dialogue.line: My ball made of celebrity hair! I've been looking everywhere for this!

dialogue.speaker: Malloy
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, it was in the evidence locker.

jokes.text: That reminds me. Here's your monthly kickback. / You just-- You couldn't have picked a worse time.
jokes.setup: Wiggum talks about law enforcement
jokes.punchline: Fat Tony gives him a bribe in front of everyone, Wiggum only objects to the timing
jokes.explanation: Good corruption humor - Wiggum's only concern is the bad optics, not the actual bribery

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: You know, being in law enforcement means upholding the highest standards of integrity and honesty.

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Here, Chief, this is for you.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa! Not in front of everybody!

jokes.text: It's buried right here in Springfield, under a big 'T.'
jokes.setup: Malloy reveals where he hid his stolen loot
jokes.punchline: Gives a vague clue that will drive everyone crazy searching
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the mass hysteria - specific enough to seem real, vague enough to be useless

dialogue.speaker: Malloy
dialogue.line: You want to know where I hid the loot? It's somewhere you'd never think to look. It's hidden in a place that's both obvious and invisible at the same time. Good luck finding it, suckers!

jokes.text: Very well. It's buried at 4723 Maple Valley Road. [Sighs] You take Highway 201 south for 15 minutes. You take a left, a left--
jokes.setup: Crowd demands more specific directions
jokes.punchline: Malloy gives incredibly detailed directions before being cut off by violence
jokes.explanation: Escalation showing Malloy's genteel nature even while being attacked - still trying to be helpful with directions

dialogue.speaker: Malloy
dialogue.line: Alright, alright! You take Main Street to the corner of Fifth and Main, then you hang a left at the traffic light — no wait, it's a right, actually it's a left past the Kwik-E-Mart, and then you'll see a big oak tree, or it might be a elm, and you count about four utility poles after that, or was it five? Anyway, you'll pass a house with blue shutters, or maybe they were green, and then—

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: *attacks*

jokes.text: What's the matter with you, kid? You told me the stream was shallow! Why, you-- I-- [Chuckling] Oh, you. Oh, kid. I'll get even with you.
jokes.setup: Malloy escapes and appears to be threatening a child
jokes.punchline: It's actually friendly banter about being tricked about water depth
jokes.explanation: Shows Malloy's gentlemanly nature - even his 'threats' are playful, treating crime like a game

dialogue.speaker: Malloy
dialogue.line: You tricked me about the water depth!

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: It's just a joke, Malloy!

dialogue.speaker: Malloy
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose that's fair. You got me good.

jokes.text: Professor, without knowing precisely what the danger is... would you say it's time for our viewers to crack each other's heads open... and feast on the goo inside? / Yes, I would, Kent.
jokes.setup: Kent asks for expert advice about the crisis
jokes.punchline: Escalates to recommending cannibalistic brain-eating, which the Professor endorses
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of the earlier panic question - takes the absurd expert advice to extreme horrific conclusion

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Professor, what do you recommend we do about this crisis?

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Well, Kent, first we try rational solutions. Then we move to irrational ones. And finally, we eat each other's brains.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Eat each other's brains?

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Yes, it's quite nutritious, and the protein content is surprisingly high. I'd start with the frontal lobe, very tender.

jokes.text: 'Frightfully sorry, but there is no hidden treasure. I have already used this time to escape from your jail. Fondest wishes--' Oh, I can't make out the signature!
jokes.setup: Everyone digs frantically for treasure and finds a note
jokes.punchline: Malloy's polite note explaining his deception, Homer can't read the signature
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the treasure hunt - Malloy's genteel politeness even in his deception, Homer's reading difficulties

dialogue.speaker: Malloy
dialogue.line: Dear friends, I regret to inform you that the treasure you seek does not exist. I merely sought to bring you together for this adventure. Sincerely, Captain Malloy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Who the hell is Captain Malloy?

jokes.text: We'll dig our way out! [Grunting] / No, no. Dig up, stupid.
jokes.setup: Everyone is trapped in the hole they dug
jokes.punchline: Homer starts digging deeper, Wiggum has to explain the obvious solution
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending - Homer's backwards logic requiring correction from equally incompetent Wiggum. Great physical comedy setup.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll dig us out of here!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Homer, no! We need to dig UP, not down.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Up? Why didn't you say so?

tmdb_overview: Homer leads a vigilante group to catch the Springfield Cat Burglar, who then robs the museum.
insight: Homer's vigilante justice spawns 65 jokes in 22 minutes—Season 5's densest episode yet.
